rationale: §165(2) sentence 1 imposes a general duty to manage risks to telecom network/service security, which is the domain-level equivalent of the ISO 27005 risk assessment process as a whole.

§165(2) requires risk management measures to be proportionate to the existing risk, considering operator size, probability, and severity — directly corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.6 context establishment which defines scope, risk criteria, and acceptance thresholds.

§165(2) mandates alignment with state-of-the-art and relevant European/international standards when scoping measures, which ISO 27005 Cl.6 addresses through context establishment including external context and regulatory requirements.

§165(2) criteria for assessing appropriateness — probability of occurrence, severity, societal/economic impact — correspond to ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 risk evaluation which compares risk levels against pre-defined risk criteria.

§165(2) specifies protecting telecoms and data-processing systems against unauthorized access as a threat category to be managed, which ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 addresses as part of risk identification (identifying threats, vulnerabilities, and risk scenarios).

§165(2) No.1 requires protection against disruptions causing significant impairments (including from external attacks and disasters), which ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 addresses through identification of risk scenarios covering availability threats.

§165(2) sentence on keeping security violation impacts minimal for users and interconnected networks maps to ISO 27005 Cl.8 risk treatment — selecting controls to reduce consequence (impact) of realized risk scenarios.

§165(2) explicitly names encryption as a required protective measure category; ISO 27005 Cl.8 covers selection of risk modification controls (including cryptographic controls) as part of the treatment process.

§165(6) defines appropriateness as cost-to-asset-value proportionality, directly mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 risk evaluation where risk levels are compared against risk acceptance criteria to determine if risk is tolerable.

§165(2a) No.1 mandates risk analysis and information security concepts as the first cross-hazard measure, directly equivalent to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification which initiates the risk assessment cycle.

§165(2a) No.1 requirement for information security concepts corresponds to ISO 27005 Cl.6 context establishment, which defines the organizational framework and criteria underpinning the ISMS risk process.

§165(2a) No.1 explicit 'Risikoanalyse' (risk analysis) requirement for KRITIS telecom operators maps directly to ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 which requires assessing likelihood and consequence to determine risk level.

§165(2a) No.2 security incident management obligation overlaps with ISO 27005 Cl.10.5 monitoring of risk-related events, as detected incidents serve as input signals for updating risk scenarios and treatment responses.

§165(2a) No.2 incident handling requires detection capability, corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.10.5 which introduces monitoring scenarios translating risk scenarios into correlation rules (e.g., SIEM detection rules).

§165(2a) No.3 business continuity and backup/disaster recovery requirement implies availability risk scenarios must be identified, corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification of availability-impacting threat scenarios.

§165(2a) No.3 BCM measures (backup, disaster recovery, crisis management) are risk treatment controls addressing availability risks, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 risk treatment process for selecting and implementing controls.

§165(2a) No.4 supply chain security obligation requires identification of risks from suppliers and service providers, directly corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification which includes threat sources from the supply chain.

§165(2a) No.4 supply chain security controls (screening suppliers, contractual requirements) are risk treatment measures addressing third-party risk, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment option selection.

§165(2a) No.5 security in system acquisition, development, and maintenance addresses the identification of risks introduced through system development lifecycle, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 identification of vulnerability-based risk scenarios.
§165(2a) No.5 vulnerability management within system maintenance maps to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification requiring assessment of vulnerabilities in network and information systems.

§165(2a) No.5 measures to secure systems during acquisition and maintenance represent risk modification controls, corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment actions to reduce risk likelihood through secure development practices.

§165(2a) No.6 requires procedures for assessing effectiveness of network and service security measures, directly equivalent to ISO 27005 Cl.10.5 monitoring of risk-related events to verify that implemented controls remain effective.

§165(2a) No.6 effectiveness evaluation cycle corresponds to ISO 27005 periodic review activities (likely Cl.10.3, exact sub-clause title unverified) which require reviewing whether risk assessment results remain valid.

§165(2a) No.7 basic security hygiene training and procedures reduces human-factor risk likelihood, which ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 risk analysis requires quantifying; training is a contributing factor to likelihood estimation.
§165(2a) No.7 security procedures represent documented risk treatment controls, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment process which selects and documents controls to modify identified risks.

§165(2a) No.8 cryptography and encryption concepts are specific risk treatment controls (modifying risk by protecting confidentiality and integrity), directly mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 risk modification through control selection.

§165(2a) No.8 encryption concepts imply identifying risks to key management and encrypted data as distinct risk scenarios, corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification for asset-based scenarios involving cryptographic assets.

§165(2a) No.9 personnel security and access control requirements presuppose identification of insider threat and access-related risk scenarios, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification for human threat sources.

§165(2a) No.9 access controls and asset management are risk treatment controls reducing unauthorized access risk, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment via risk modification by implementing access management controls.

§165(2a) No.10 multi-factor authentication is a specific risk treatment control targeting authentication bypass threats, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 risk modification through technical control selection.

§165(2a) No.10 secured voice/video/text communications and emergency communication systems are confidentiality risk treatment controls, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment options for data-in-transit risk scenarios.

§165(3) intrusion detection system requirement for high-risk operators maps precisely to ISO 27005 Cl.10.5 monitoring of risk-related events, translating risk scenarios into detection rules for continuous operational monitoring.

§165(3) triggers mandatory IDS deployment for operators with elevated risk, implying a risk evaluation threshold has been crossed — corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 risk evaluation comparing assessed risk level against acceptance criteria.

§165(4) pre-deployment certification of critical network components addresses risks from unvetted components (supply chain integrity), corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 identification of risk scenarios involving critical asset vulnerabilities.

§165(4) certification requirement for critical components is a risk treatment control (risk modification through procurement requirements), mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment actions including contractual and procurement controls.

§165(5) obligation to ensure service availability through operational measures requires identification of availability risk scenarios including cascading failure and overload threats, mapping to ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 risk identification.

§165(5) operational availability measures are risk treatment controls (risk modification) targeting service disruption risk scenarios, corresponding to ISO 27005 Cl.8 treatment option selection and implementation.

§165(2b) management board implementation and monitoring responsibility aligns with ISO 27005 Cl.6 context establishment requiring leadership commitment and accountability for the risk management process.

§165(2c) management liability for negligent security measure violations corresponds to accountability mechanisms in ISO 27005 documentation requirements (likely Cl.10.4, exact sub-clause unverified) ensuring documented evidence of risk decisions.

§165(2d) mandatory cybersecurity training for management reduces organizational risk likelihood, which ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 risk analysis must factor in when estimating likelihood of human-factor risk scenarios.

§165(9) (UNVERIFIED numbering — biennial audit requirement for high-risk operators) maps to ISO 27005 Cl.10.5 monitoring process requiring periodic review and update of risk assessments to capture changed threat landscape.

TKG §165 as a whole imposes a sector-specific risk-based security obligation that is broader in regulatory scope (covering telecom-specific threats, proportionality, enforcement) than ISO 27005 Cl.7 alone, making the TKG a superset relative to the assessment process.

§165(2a) gefahrenübergreifender Ansatz (cross-hazard approach covering physical and cyber threats) expands the risk scope beyond ISO 27005 Cl.6 context establishment which sets organizational and information-system scope but does not explicitly mandate cross-domain physical threat inclusion.

§165(2) paragraph requiring risk-appropriate security level considering probability, severity, and societal/economic impact as evaluation criteria is functionally equivalent to ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 which evaluates assessed risk against criteria including likelihood, impact magnitude, and organizational risk appetite.

§165(2a) No.1 risk analysis concepts encompass the full assessment loop (identification → analysis → evaluation), which as a unit corresponds to ISO 27005 Cl.7 information security risk assessment process.
